data.title: Thob Al Aseel Posts Growth in FY24 Net Profit; Revenue Drops
data.description:
Thob Al Aseel (SASE:4012) said Sunday that profit for full-year 2024 increased, while revenue slipped year over year.

Net profit for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 83.9 million Saudi riyals, compared with 76.9 million riyals earlier. EPS moved to 0.21 riyal from 0.19 riyal.

The Saudi Arabia-based garment manufacturer's revenue was 522.5 million riyals, compared with 532.2 million riyals a year ago.

Meanwhile, the board declared an unchanged cash dividend of 0.1 riyal per share for the second half of 2024, payable within 15 business days to shareholders on record Monday.

data.title: Al Mohafaza Co. for Education Secures License for School Operations in Saudi Arabia
data.description:
Al Mohafaza Co. for Education (SASE:9598) secured a school operations license from Saudi's General Organization for Technical and Vocational Training.

The license will allow the Saudi Arabian school company to conduct training diplomas, qualification programs, and development programs at its Al-Muhafaza Higher Institute for Training facility in Saudi Arabia, according to a Sunday release.

data.title: Uganda reports second Ebola death, a 4-year-old, WHO says
data.description:
A second Ebola patient, a four-year-old child, has died in Uganda, the World Health Organization said, citing the country’s health ministry.

The fatality brings the number of confirmed cases in Uganda to 10.

The East African country declared an outbreak of the highly infectious and often fatal haemorrhagic disease in January after the death of a male nurse at the Mulago National Referral Hospital in the capital Kampala.

The WHO's Uganda office posted late on Saturday on X that the ministry had reported "an additional positive case in Mulago hospital of a four-and-a-half-year-old child, who tragically passed away" on Tuesday.

Mulago is the country's sole national referral hospital for Ebola cases.

The ministry said on February 18 that all eight Ebola patients under care had been discharged but that at least 265 contacts remained under strict quarantine in Kampala and two other cities.

Ebola symptoms include fever, headache and muscle pains. The virus is transmitted through contact with infected bodily fluids and tissue.

data.title: Abdullah Al Othaim Markets to Buy 51% Stake in Ebdaa Al Qasr Marketing
data.description:
Abdullah Al Othaim Markets (SASE:4001) entered into a nonbinding agreement to purchase a 51% stake in food and consumer goods retailer Ebdaa Al Qasr Marketing.

The retail company's 90-day renewable memorandum of understanding is subject to share valuation, due diligence, and regulatory approval, according to a Sunday release.

data.title: Ericsson-led Aduna, Bridge Alliance Partner for Application Programming Interfaces
data.description:
Ericsson's (ERIC-B.ST, ERIC-A.ST) Aduna venture with global telecom companies will collaborate with Bridge Alliance to boost the adoption of Camara-based network application programming interfaces, according to a March 1 release.

Aduna and the mobile alliance in the Asia Pacific, Middle East, and Africa will also team up in support of critical global standardization efforts, the Swedish telecom equipment maker said.

data.title: Saudi Tadawul Group Posts Growth in FY24 Profit, Revenue
data.description:
Saudi Tadawul Group (SASE:1111) said Sunday that profit and revenue for full-year 2024 grew year over year.

Net profit for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 621.8 million Saudi riyals, compared with 390.1 million riyals earlier. EPS moved to 5.18 riyals from 3.25 riyals.

The Saudi bourse's revenue was 1.45 billion riyals, compared with 1.07 billion riyals a year ago.

Meanwhile, the board proposed a dividend of 3.35 riyals per share for the period, with eligibility and distribution dates disclosed later. It paid 2.30 riyals per share a year ago.

data.title: Autoneum Concludes Acquisition of Majority Stake in Chinese Automotive Supplier
data.description:
Autoneum Holding closed the planned purchase of a 70% interest in Chinese automotive supplier Jiangsu Huanyu Group after securing regulatory approvals.

The maker of components for light and commercial vehicles, which operates 13 production facilities, will retain its Chinese name. The Swiss automotive parts manufacturer has the option to buy the remaining 30% of Jiangsu Huanyu in 2028.

data.title: J&J Seeks $9 Billion Settlement to End Unit's 15-year Talc Litigation
data.description:
Johnson & Johnson is looking to secure a $9 billion settlement to end a 15-year lawsuit over claims that the company's talc powder caused cancer.

The proposal was followed after its Red River Talc unit held a pre-bankruptcy vote in 2024, in which 80% of talc claimants voted in favor of the settlement, Bloomberg News reported March 1. The vote would see the payment of $120,000 for each victim.

Other claimants, however, said that the vote was not credible and not handled properly.

data.title: Emaar The Economic City Offsets Accumulated Losses via Capital Reduction
data.description:
Emaar The Economic City (SASE:4220) cut its accumulated losses to 0% via a capital decrease, according to a Feb. 27 release.

The real estate developer canceled 610,073,425 shares, resulting in its capital to decline to 5.23 billion Saudi riyals from 11.33 billion riyals.

data.title: India's Adani Group revives US investment plans, FT reports
data.description:
India's Adani Group has revived plans for major infrastructure investments in the U.S., where the group's founder has been charged with bribery, the Financial Times reported on Sunday.

Since the election of President Donald Trump, the conglomerate has reactivated potential plans to fund projects in sectors such as nuclear power and utilities, as well as an East Coast port, the report said, citing four people close to founder and chair Gautam Adani.

Federal prosecutors in New York unsealed an indictment in November accusing Gautam Adani of bribing Indian officials to persuade them to buy electricity produced by Adani Green Energy .

"We know what we want to do, but we will wait until this (case) resolves," the FT quoted a person close to Adani as saying.

Adani Group has said the charges were "baseless" and that it would seek "all possible legal recourse." It did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment on the FT report.

The group had previously been in talks with U.S. companies on potential partnerships and had looked at petrochemical investments in Texas, the newspaper said.

After Trump's November election win, Gautam Adani said the group planned to invest $10 billion in U.S. energy security and infrastructure projects, creating a potential 15,000 jobs.

Trump has vowed to make it easier for energy companies to drill on federal land and build pipelines.

"Once Trump came in, we have reactivated some plans," the FT said, citing another source it did not name.

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission asked Indian authorities last month for help in its investigation of Gautam Adani and his nephew Sagar Adani over allegations of securities fraud and a $265-million bribery scheme.

In 2023 the conglomerate was accused by U.S.-based short-seller Hindenburg Research, which disbanded earlier this year, of improper use of offshore tax havens and stock manipulation that sparked a $150 billion rout in shares of the group's companies. Adani denied those allegations.

data.title: Julius Baer's Board Picks Former HSBC CEO for Chair Role
data.description:
Julius Baer Group's board will propose the appointment of Noel Quinn, HSBC's former chief executive officer as its nonexecutive chair on April 10.

Once elected at the Swiss private banking group's annual general meeting, Quinn will take charge on May 1, according to a Feb. 28 filing. In the interim, Vice Chairman Richard Campbell-Breeden will assume the role of chairman until April 30.

Quinn's predecessor, Romeo Lacher, previously decided not to seek re-election at the April 10 annual general meeting.

data.title: AXA Sets Launch Date for Planned EUR1.2 Billion Share Buyback Program
data.description:
AXA's planned share buyback program worth up to 1.2 billion euros will begin on Monday, according to a repurchase agreement signed with an investment services company on Feb. 28.

The French insurer will cancel the shares that are repurchased under the program ending on June 30 at the latest.

data.title: ATFX, IG Group, Trive South Africa, and More: Executive Moves of the Week
data.description:
Reviewing the most notable executive moves this week: ATFX hired FXCM's Paresh Patel as the Global Head of Risk and Trading, IG Group's Head of Brand and Product Marketing exited after more than three years, Trive South Africa named Jimmy Moyaha as Non-Executive Director, while CMC Markets’ CFO Albert Soleiman is stepping down.

ATFX Hires FXCM's Paresh Patel as Global Head of Risk and Trading

Among the notable executive changes in the industry this week, the global fintech broker ATFX enlisted Paresh Patel as the Global Head of Risk and Trading, according to Patel's announcement on LinkedIn. He joined the company from FXCM, where he served as the global head of trading for more than fifteen years.

Last year, AFTX also made significant leadership changes as part of its expansion plan globally. Among the changes, the company announced the appointment of John Bogue as the Director of Institutional Operations. With over 30 years of experience in the FX market, John is experienced in trading, prime brokerage, and e-brokerage to ATFX.

Show more about ATFX's enlisting of FXCM's Former executive Paresh Patel as Global Head of Risk and Trading.

IG Group's Head of Brand and Product Marketing Departs

Leigh-Anne Acquisto, IG Group's Head of Brand and Product Marketing, is leaving the company after dedicating nearly three and a half years. Recently, Acquisto served as the Head of Brand and Product Marketing, a position she dedicated eleven months.Leigh-Anne Acquisto, Source: X

The veteran industry expert joined IG in 2021 as the Global Brand Manager and later as the Head of Brand Expression and Activation. Previously, she was the Chairperson of Brand Council South Africa, based in Johannesburg, South Africa.

Highlight more about the exit of IG Group's Head of Brand and Product Marketing Leigh-Anne Acquisto.

Trive South Africa Names Jimmy Moyaha as Non-Executive Director

Elsewhere, Trive South Africa appointed Jimmy Moyaha as its new Non-Executive Director, adding a seasoned financial expert to its leadership team. Moyaha is the current Founder and Managing Director of Lebowa Capital.Jimmy Moyaha, Source: LinkedIn

Moyaha, a well-known figure in South Africa’s financial sector, brings years of regulatory and strategic experience. He currently manages Lotus Academy as the Founder. According to his LinkedIn profile, this academy is an online educational platform for financial instruments.

Disclose more about Trive South Africa's onboarding of Jimmy Moyaha as Non-Executive Director.

CMC Markets’ CFO Albert Soleiman Steps Down

In another major executive move, the Chief Financial Officer of CMC Markets, Albert Soleiman, is stepping down from his role with immediate effect. The London-headquartered broker announced this week, adding that he will no longer be a director of the company.Albert Soleiman, Source: LinkedIn

“On behalf of CMC Markets plc, I would like to thank our outgoing CFO, Albert Soleiman, for his contributions to the firm over his long tenure with the company and in his roles as CFO and as a director. I wish him the very best in his new endeavors,” said Lord Peter Cruddas, CEO of CMC.

Disclose more about Albert Soleiman's exit from his role as the CFO of CMC Markets.

data.title: Bybit hackers resume laundering activities, moving another 62,200 ETH
data.description:
North Korea’s Lazarus Group laundered another 62,200 Ether, worth $138 million, from the Bybit hack on March 1 — leaving only 156,500 left to be moved, a pseudonymous crypto analyst noted.

Approximately 343,000 Ether of the 499,000 Ether stolen from the $1.4 billion Bybit hack has been moved, said X user EmberCN, who expects the remaining funds to be cleared in the next three days.

The 343,000 Ether moved equates to 68.7% of the stolen funds — up from 54% on Feb. 28.

EmberCN previously noted that laundering activities had slowed on Feb. 28 amid efforts from the US Federal Bureau of Investigation calling on node operators, crypto exchanges, bridges and others to block transactions linked to the Bybit hackers.

The FBI shared 51 Ethereum addresses operated by, or linked to, the Bybit hackers, while blockchain analytics firm Elliptic has flagged over 11,000 crypto wallet addresses possibly linked to them.

Crypto forensics firm Chainalysis said the hackers had converted portions of the stolen Ether into Bitcoin (BTC), the Dai (DAI) stablecoin and other assets through decentralized exchanges, crosschain bridges and instant swap services without Know Your Customer protocols.

One of those protocols includes crosschain asset swap protocol THORChain. Developers behind the protocol have received heavy criticism for facilitating a significant share of illicit transfers made by the North Korean hackers.

One of THORChain’s developers, known as “Pluto,” said they would no longer contribute to the protocol after a vote to block North Korean hacker-linked transactions was reverted.

In a note to Cointelegraph, THORChain’s founder John-Paul Thorbjornsen said he no longer has involvement with the crosschain protocol, while pointing out that none of the sanctioned crypto wallet addresses listed by the FBI and the Treasury’s Office of Foreign Assets Control have interacted with the protocol.

The $1.4 billion Bybit hack on Feb. 21 was by far the largest exploit in crypto industry — more than doubling losses from the $650 million Ronin bridge hack on March 23, 2022.

data.title: MIDEAST - Factors to watch - March 2
data.description:
